files.SKILL.md:
# Engineering Operating Protocol

*You are the sole editor of this workspace — nothing appears or disappears without your
action. What follows layers a **remote-session shell** on top of that: land a coded change
under a tight tool-call budget. Every section is a stage or rule.*

## Tool-call discipline

- Before you emit a tool call, identify the full set of calls that do not depend on each
  other given what you know **now**. Send them in one assistant message with multiple
  `tool_calls` — single-call-per-turn quietly burns the budget.
- Serialize only when a later call truly needs data from an earlier one.
- When results return, **re-bucket** for the next wave.
- Tool calls are the deliverable; long chat is not. A lean session chains a few purposeful
  waves, not dozens of one-off pings.

## The single invocation rule (read every session)

**Every shell action you take is `ssh ... 'CMD'` — the command wrapped as a single
argument to ssh. No exceptions.**

The work lives on a remote machine. Any path you read, list, write, or test must reach it
through `ssh user@host 'CMD'`. Direct path operations on the agent host do not.

```
WRONG: <direct path read of workspace files>     — wrong machine, paths look empty
WRONG: ssh user@host                              — no command, immediate exit
WRONG: cat /workspace/SKILL.md                    — runs locally, file not there
RIGHT: ssh user@host 'cat /workspace/SKILL.md'    — reads the real workspace
```

If a path comes back empty, recover on the next call with a proper wrap. Two identical
failures → change mechanism, not a third repeat.

- **Every workspace action goes through ssh** as one non-interactive command string. The
  default shell is your agent container — not the repo.
- Do NOT use IDE file tools (`read_file`, search, view, …) for **remote workspace paths** —
  they read the wrong filesystem. Use `ssh ... 'cat'` / `ssh ... 'ls'`.
- A bare `ssh user@host` with no command is a no-op; the next call may run on the wrong
  host.
- Commit when the visible suite passes. Partial committed beats polished uncommitted.

## Reads in parallel, writes in serial

- Independent reads → one batch when paths are knowable upfront.
- If a layout document exists, read it first; then only paths it authorises — do not pair
  it with speculative reads.
- Writes: change → verify → continue; do not overlap writes on the same artefact.

## Startup

- **One** combined listing — workspace, source, tests, branches, **state** directories. Broad
  commands; no narrow filters that hide the map. Do not reach for paths you have not seen;
  do not re-read what the prompt already holds.
- Trust that listing for this pass: if a name is not in it, it is not there to open yet.
- **One** parallel batch of reads for files the ticket names, retained records, notes, named
  sources — stop the moment you can act.
- Branch from the latest **task** branch that already carries this thread.

## Common task shapes

Use ticket names verbatim.

- **Build** — satisfy the visible contract, then commit.
- **Replicate** — thin wiring; fold any open failure on that seam into the same commit.
- **Fix** — defect class + records → one surgical edit, named branch, commit.
- **Document** — no code churn; cite identifiers verbatim at the named path.

## Engineering standards

- Stick to the requested feature. Note out-of-scope defects instead of silent patches.
  Within the fence: **production-ready**, not a sketch.
- Add files only when asked; tests only for the requested change; no unrelated churn.
- When state expires in time, key by the **time axis** — whole intervals age out together.
- Before extending or debugging prior work, read **records** first (tests, commits, configs,
  notes). Reading beats reverse-engineering.
- Design for growth and for failure (bounds, races, bad inputs).

Where the ticket and visible checks do not forbid a concern, the first commit should also
respect:

- bounded retained state and release of resources when work ends;
- bounded hot-path cost vs internal state size;
- guarded read–decide–write on shared mutable state;
- monotonic timing for durations when clocks can step;
- explicit inclusive / exclusive boundary choices beside comparisons.

A defect named in writing but not fixed in code still ships.

## Git

- Set `user.name` / `user.email` before the first commit — missing identity blocks commits
  silently.
- `git add <path>`, never `-A`.
- Single-line commit messages.
- No amend, rebase, or squash.
- The first noisy commit adds `.gitignore` once (`__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.pytest_cache/`).

## Writing files

**Compose locally → `scp` → read back with `cat`.** Heredocs and `python3 -c` eat quotes and
`$`. If a heredoc is unavoidable, use a **quoted** delimiter (`<< 'EOF'`). Where `scp` is
unavailable, a small scratch script with a triple-quoted payload is the fallback. Single-
token edits: `sed -i` on the remote — do not re-upload a whole file to flip one operator.

**When a write fails, switch tactic** by the third try — never loop the same broken pattern.

## Learning

- Notes capture **stable** observations — tooling quirks, broken assumptions, recurring shapes.
- **First line** of your scratch area anchors the **ssh wrap** invariant; below that, only
  verified run facts.
- Write at the moment a surprise lands; one verified line each; modify in place; live data
  wins over stale notes.
- Skip generic lore, ephemeral IDs or paths, anything `git log` already teaches.

## The deliverable is a triple

Running change + commit + durable note when something non-obvious was learned. If most of
the budget is gone before the first edit, cut reads and **land** something small. When
continuing prior work, reference the parent commit hash in the message.

## Exit

Visible tests pass, commit exists, notes updated when warranted — then exit. A summary
without a commit is not delivery.
